title: Tirokafteri
subtitle: Greek spicy feta dip
excerpt: Feta crumbled and beaten with roasted red pepper, chilli, and olive oil into a sharp, fiery dip. Ready in five minutes and vastly better than anything sold in a tub.
sourceNotes: Tirokafteri (from tiri, cheese, and kafteri, hot/spicy) is a standard Greek mezze dip. Documented in Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001). The addition of roasted red pepper to soften the heat is common in northern Greece.

content.text: Tirokafteri is the fiery one on the mezze table, the counterpoint to tzatziki's coolness. The heat comes from chilli flakes, the sharpness from feta, and a roasted red pepper rounds the whole thing out. The result is bright pink, salty, and fiercely hot.

The texture is up to you. Crumbled and roughly mixed it stays chunky; beaten hard or processed briefly it becomes smooth and almost spreadable. Both are correct. A spoonful of Greek yoghurt stirred in at the end tames the heat slightly and makes the dip creamier, optional, but worth considering if the chilli is strong.

items.name: Red pepper
items.prepNote: roasted, peeled, and roughly chopped (or 60 g from a jar)

content.text: If using a fresh pepper, char it directly over a gas flame or under a very hot grill, turning until the skin is blackened all over. Put in a bowl, cover with a plate, and leave for 10 minutes. Peel away the charred skin, remove the seeds, and chop roughly. A jar of roasted red peppers works perfectly well here.

Put the crumbled feta, roasted pepper, chilli flakes, olive oil, and lemon juice into a bowl. If using garlic, crush it to a paste and add it. Mix with a fork, mashing as you go, until the feta breaks down into a rough paste. For a smoother result, use the back of a spoon or pulse briefly in a food processor. Stir in the yoghurt if using. Taste, add more chilli, lemon, or oil as needed. Serve with warm pitta and a drizzle of olive oil.

items.fix: Add the yoghurt to dilute the salt, or squeeze in a little more lemon juice to balance
items.cause: Feta varies in salinity; some blocks are much saltier than others

items.fix: Add more chilli flakes or a small pinch of cayenne
items.cause: Chilli flakes vary in heat depending on the variety

items.fix: Tirokafteri is naturally somewhat coarse; aim for a rough paste, not a smooth cream, crumble the feta by hand and stir rather than blending

content.text: Tirokafteri sits alongside melitzanosalata and taramasalata on the mezze table. All three are made in minutes, all three are served with pitta, and all three share the same idea: make something from very little. The name is direct: tiri (cheese) plus kafteri (hot). That covers everything you need to know.
